findings.description: Full card number and CVV are written to application logs in plaintext, violating PCI-DSS which forbids persisting CVV anywhere.
charge_order never checks whether the order is already 'paid', so a retry or concurrent call double-charges the customer.
refund only flips the DB status to 'refunded' and never calls the payment gateway, so customer funds are never actually returned.
The success UPDATE sets status and tx_id but never sets paid_at, so monthly_revenue_report's paid_at filter silently excludes every order charged through this code.
Revenue is computed as raw unit_price*quantity, ignoring discount_pct and tax, so revenue_cents disagrees with the amounts actually charged in charge_order.
No idempotency key is sent to the gateway and a crash or commit failure between gateway.charge and conn.commit leaves the customer charged while the order stays unpaid.
discount_pct is unvalidated, so negative values silently inflate the price and values over 100 produce negative line totals.
Binary floats are used for money throughout (unit_price, TAX_RATE, subtotals), causing cumulative rounding drift versus Decimal or integer cents.

Transaction IDs derived from hash() of (last4, amount) collide for identical inputs and are not deterministic across processes due to string hash randomization, contradicting the 'deterministic stub' comment.
refund's check-then-update is not atomic (UPDATE lacks AND status='paid'), so concurrent calls can both pass the check and double-refund.
N+1 query pattern: order_items are fetched per order inside a loop instead of one JOIN/GROUP BY aggregate.
strftime() applied to the paid_at column makes the predicate non-sargable, forcing a full table scan on large orders tables.
Filtering on status='paid' means refunded orders retroactively vanish from past months' revenue reports, distorting historical accounting.
The application handles raw PAN and CVV strings instead of gateway tokens, placing this code fully in PCI-DSS scope and increasing breach impact.
int(round(total*100)) applies banker's rounding to an imprecise float product, which can mischarge by one cent at half-cent boundaries.
The gateway rejects amount_cents <= 0, so a legitimately free order (100% discount or zero items) can never transition to 'paid'.
SELECT * hides the schema dependency on discount_pct and fetches unneeded columns; explicit column lists are safer.
refund returns a bare bool that conflates 'order not found' with 'not in paid status', leaving callers unable to distinguish failure reasons.
compute_order_total returns an unrounded float total despite the 'Total in dollars' contract, so external callers may re-round inconsistently with charge_order.
year/month are unvalidated, so out-of-range values like month=13 silently return an empty report instead of raising an error.
